coredata.dc:title: A facile and green method towards coal-based fluorescent carbon dots with photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Shengliang
Wei, Zhijia
Chang, Qing
Trinchi, Adrian
Yang, Jinlong
coredata.dc:description: One of the most widely used methods for exfoliating crystalline nanocarbon is via strong oxidizing acid treatment of bulk carbon sources, such as graphite, carbon black and coal. Not only is such method dangerous and accompanied by the liberation of toxic gases, it is also plagued by issues of purity, requiring the thorough and costly removal of the excess oxidizing acids and salts formed during the process. Herein we report a facile, green and inexpensive top-down strategy towards fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from coal without incurring the burden of tedious or inefficient post-processing steps and facing the danger of highly toxic gas liberation. The presented approach shows a high yield and great potential for carbon dot production scale-up using coal, one of our most abundant and low-cost resources. The prepared CDs demonstrate photocatalytic behavior capable of rapidly degrading organic dyes under visible light. Our findings may lead to alternative uses of coal, particularly for applications including the treatment of environmental pollution, solar energy conversion or storage, and highlight coal’s applicability in areas other than energy producing via burning of this great resource.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Coal chemistry
Photocatalysis
Luminescence

U1510125 , 51272301 , 51172214 , 51502270 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (Nos. 2012M510788 , 2013T60269 ), Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths ( 2014021008 ), 131 Talent Plan of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of coal and the formed CDs. (a) SEM image of ball-milled coal; (b) TEM image of CDs; (c) the corresponding HRTEM of CDs; (d) EDS of CDs; (e) PL spectra of CDs excited at different wavelengths.

Formation mechanism of CDs from coal. (a) Schematic illustration of forming CDs by selectively oxidizing organic carbon in coal; (b) time-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of TAOH formed by the reaction of TA with OH generated by reaction system under the heating at 80°C (left) and the time-dependent fluorescence intensities of TAOH at 426nm (right).
Chemical composition characterization of CDs. (a) high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum; (b) high-resolution XPS O1s spectrum; (c) FTIR spectrum.

Comparative photocatalytic activity of CDs and TiO2. Photocatalytic degradation of MB (a) and MO (b) in the presence of commercial P25 TiO2 and the prepared CDs under the visible light irradiation; (c) schematic illustration of photocatalytic process from CDs; (d) time-dependent fluorescence intensities of TAOH formed by the reaction of TA with OH generated by CDs and P25, respectively, under the visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: A facile and green method towards coal-based fluorescent carbon dots with photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIRO Materials Science and Engineering, I Private Bag 33, Clayton 3169, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One of the most widely used methods for exfoliating crystalline nanocarbon is via strong oxidizing acid treatment of bulk carbon sources, such as graphite, carbon black and coal. Not only is such method dangerous and accompanied by the liberation of toxic gases, it is also plagued by issues of purity, requiring the thorough and costly removal of the excess oxidizing acids and salts formed during the process. Herein we report a facile, green and inexpensive top-down strategy towards fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from coal without incurring the burden of tedious or inefficient post-processing steps and facing the danger of highly toxic gas liberation. The presented approach shows a high yield and great potential for carbon dot production scale-up using coal, one of our most abundant and low-cost resources. The prepared CDs demonstrate photocatalytic behavior capable of rapidly degrading organic dyes under visible light. Our findings may lead to alternative uses of coal, particularly for applications including the treatment of environmental pollution, solar energy conversion or storage, and highlight coal’s applicability in areas other than energy producing via burning of this great resource.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their pronounced quantum confinement and edge effects, zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, named as carbon dots (CDs), possess numerous novel chemical/physical properties, making them promising in photo- and electro-catalysis, bioimaging, chemical sensing, and for optoelectronic devices, etc [1–12]. To obtain fluorescent CDs, tremendous effort has been placed on developing synthetic methods. Top-down approaches have proven to be very effective, and take advantage of the low cost and highly abundant raw materials [13–20]. They can also facilitate large scale production and have simple operation. Synthesis of CDs by pyrolysis or carbonization of small organic molecules, fruits, or plants has also been widely reported [2,3,5,10–12,21–23]. Nevertheless, these bottom-up methods typically involve complex synthetic procedures, utilize a variety of toxic and volatile organic solvents, can require costly catalysts to drive the condensation reactions, and specialty organic precursors that may be difficult to obtain [2,3,5].

Treatment with strong oxidizing agents (e.g. HNO3, H2SO4, and KMnO4) has been widely employed to exfoliate CDs from carbon black, activated carbon, carbon fibers, graphene oxide, soot and coal [14–20]. This method is amenable for large-scale solution-processing from readily available low-cost carbon sources. However, the major limiting factor preventing industrial scale production of high quality CDs using this approach has been the inherent difficulty in thoroughly removing both the excess oxidizing agents and the inorganic salts that form during the neutralization phase via the addition of bases. Additionally, this treatment process is dangerous, employing strong oxidizing agents and the reactions are always accompanied by the liberation of highly toxic gases. Consequently, in order for the successful large scale production of these CDs to come to fruition, it is essential that green and facile CD production strategies are developed.

Coal is mainly used as a combustible energy resource due to its structural characteristics [16,17]. It contains nanometer-sized crystalline carbon domains that are joined by aliphatic amorphous carbon and polymerized aromatic hydrocarbon [16]. Such small crystalline domains are abundant within coal and meet the quantum dot size. If these domains can be exfoliated by an effective strategy, then coal becomes an ideal carbon source for preparing CDs. Ye et al. reported acidic exfoliation of coal using concentrated H2SO4 and HNO3 similar with graphene oxide preparation from graphite, though this method suffers from disadvantages mentioned above [16].

In this work, we have developed a novel strategy for producing high quality coal-derived fluorescent CDs via a selective oxidation reaction, which utilizes active hydroxyl radicals (OH) from H2O2 to remove organic amorphous carbon in coal on the basis of the different stability of crystalline carbon and organic carbon. This method is green, facile, safe and effective without incurring the burden of toxic gas emission or the need for stringent, tedious, intricate, or costly purification steps. In particular, the obtained CDs also show greater photocatalytic performance in organic degradation than commercial TiO2 (P25) under visible light irradiation, making them a promising substitute for photocatalysts comprised of scarce, expensive, or toxic elements.

Anthracite was used without further treatment and obtained from Yangquan Coal Industry, China. A four-station horizontal planetary mill was used for reducing the size of coal. The initial powder, 1g anthracite and 20mL H2O were loaded in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined milling vial with 25 ceramic balls, which are composed of 8 large balls with a diameter of 10mm and 17 small balls with a diameter of 6mm. The vial was then fixed in the planetary ball-mill machine, and agitated at 400rpm for 5h. The resultant product was dried at 60°C in vacuum oven. 200mg ultrafine powder was dispersed in 10mL H2O by ultrasonication and then mixed with 20mL of 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution. The obtained mixture was heated at 80°C in the atmosphere. After reacting for 10min under vigorous stirring conditions, another 5mL of 30% H2O2 solution was added. This process was repeated four times within 40min. The total volume of H2O2 added in the reaction system was 40mL. The reaction time reached 3h after which the experiment was stopped. The finial product was obtained by removing unreacted coal via centrifugation at 3500rpm and the excess H2O2 was removed through solution boiling.

Coal samples were first ball milled to reduce their particle size [24], with scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images revealing the size reduced significantly to several micrometers (Fig. 1 a) and the particles assumed flake-like shapes. The significant increase in their specific surface area creates more structural defects in the coal and high activity capable of almost complete contact with the H2O2 reactant used subsequently for selective oxidation. Treatment of the ball-milled coal with the H2O2 solution resulted in a dark yellow suspension containing CDs (Fig. S1). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images indicate the nanoparticles in suspension are mainly distributed from 1 to 3nm in diameter (Fig. 1b). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals that the nanoparticle has crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of ca. 0.21nm, which is close to the (100) diffraction facets of graphite carbon (Fig. 1c). Additionally, Raman spectroscopy was employed to characterize the intrinsic structure of the CDs (Fig. S2). The G band peaking at 1585cm−1 indicates sp2 carbon networks and the D band peaking at 1346cm−1 reflects the defects or disorder in the graphitized structure. The existence of D bands could mainly result from surface oxidation and small size of CDs. The corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) spectrum suggests there are mainly C and O elements. Si is also observed, likely from impurities within the coal sample (Table S1), and Cu elements arise from the copper TEM grid.

Fig. 1e shows photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the carbon dot suspension excited at the different wavelengths. The 455nm peak at has a large full width at half maximum (FWHM), corresponding to cyan fluorescence (Fig. S3). The time-resolved PL decay exhibits a three-exponential function with lifetimes of 1.14ns (20.7%), 4.27ns (51.6%), and 10.26ns (27.7%) at 375nm excitation, with an intensity-weighted average time of 5.28ns (Fig. S4). These multiple lifetimes suggest the presence of diverse fluorophores or energy levels in CDs [25], which are probably responsible for the large FWHM and excited-wavelength-dependent PL behaviors from CDs. The obtained CDs show broad optical absorption from UV to visible light region, with no obvious peaks observed (Fig. S5). This is likely due to the distribution of different electron transitions.

PL mechanism of CDs has been discussed extensively [4,22,25–30]. An increasingly accepted viewpoint is radiative recombination of the surface-confined electrons and holes [4,25,28–30], which is different from those traditional semiconductor quantum dots. Therefore, there is not band gap in the optical absorption and PL emission of CDs, whereas their surface functionalized groups can influence and tune surface-state emission due to the formation of localized electronic states. For instance, the theoretical calculations and experimental results have shown that the band gap tuning or PL shifting of CDs could be associated with the degree of oxidation and types of surface oxygen-containing groups, and the introduction of amine-groups [22,23,25,26,28–33]. A verified experiment was performed with the use of ethylenediamine molecules for coal-based CD surface functionalization. The PL emission of the obtained CDs shifts to red region, emitting green light at 375nm excitation (Fig. S6). This result is consistent with previous reports [26,32].

The CD yield was estimated in two ways. In the first approach, the suspension of CDs obtained after reacting for 3h at 80°C was dried using vacuum freeze-drying equipment. The ratio between the obtained CD solid powder and the amount of coal used was ca. 10%. According to previous reports, CDs produced by strong acid treatment of coal required up to 24h processing time to achieve a yield of to 20% [16]. Under the same conditions, our presented method is capable of producing eight times more CDs than these previous approaches. In our approach, the unreacted residues, which assume black color (Fig. S7a), contain excess coal. Continually adding H2O2 to the reaction system converted the remaining carbon to CDs, leaving behind a grey/white, which was identified as impurities from within the coal, e.g. alumina and silica (See coal composion in Table S1). These did not react with H2O2 (Fig. S7b). The second approach for CD yield assessment is the ratio between the CD powder and the amount of the coal excluding the solid impurities. In this case the yield was determined to be in the range of 50–60%.

The CD formation mechanism is depicted in Fig. 2 a. From coal’s microstructure, the crystalline domains are connected by organic carbon chains. Now the OH radical, which is considered one of the most powerful oxidizing species [34–36], can selectively oxidize the organic carbon chains into CO2 and H2O when heated, leaving behind stable crystalline carbon and thus formation of CDs. This supposition was supported by HRTEM images in the presence of organic carbon surrounding crystalline carbon (Fig. S8), and which has also been observed previously [16]. H2O2 can dissociate into OH in water, and the progress of OH formation was probed by terephthalic acid (TA), which is well known for reacting with OH to generate 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) [37–39]. TAOH also emits a unique fluorescence signal whose peak is centered at ca. 426nm when excited at 312nm. Importantly, it is noted that the fluorescence emission from carbon dot suspensions obtained within 0.5h reaction time is very weak in contrast with the amount formed TAOH (Fig. S9). Thus the fluorescence signal from the formed TAOH can be clearly distinguished from CDs in the short reaction time. From Fig. 2b the significant fluorescence signals associated with TAOH were produced upon heating coal suspended in the mixed solution of TA and H2O2 at 80°C for different reaction times. This result strongly indicates that the thermal excitation energy can be efficiently transferred to surface absorbed water and coal’s hydroxyl groups to generate OH. The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and reaction time implies the stability of the reaction system composed of coal powders and H2O2.

Factors influencing this reaction system are the reaction temperature, reaction time and the added amount of H2O2. With all other conditions remaining unaltered, CDs derived at different reaction temperatures shows similar emission spectra but vastly different PL intensities. A nonlinear relationship between PL intensities and reaction temperatures is observed, and the strongest PL emission occurs from CDs produced at 80°C (Fig. S10). Low temperatures suppress the ability of transferring H2O2 to OH due to insufficient energy while too high temperatures facilitate the decomposition of H2O2 to O2, consequently reducing the OH generation. Accordingly, the reaction temperature could cause the change of the amount of OH in reaction system, affecting CD. According to Fig. 2b, increasing the reaction time induces enhancement of OH numbers, theoretically making for more products. However, the OH could attack the carbon atoms of crystalline nanoparticles containing hydroxyl and epoxide groups, thus consuming those CDs which were formed. The measured PL intensities from CDs increases with reaction time, whereas the lower intensities were observed when the reaction ran too long (Fig. S11). It is clear that in order to maximize the PL intensity of the CDs, an appropriate amount of H2O2 needs to be added to the reaction CD system (in this case 40mL) and the reaction temperature also requires optimization (again in this case 80°C) (Fig. S12). On the other hand, monitoring the OH fluorescence shows there is not a linear relationship between the amount of H2O2 and the number of generated OH (Fig. S13).

The observed chemical composition at the surface of the obtained CDs also supported the formation mechanism of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results showed distinct peaks at ca. 286.5eV and 288.5eV corresponding to CO and COOH groups (Fig. 3 a) [22]. In particular, the intensity of the peak assigned to the COOH group is very strong. The XPS O1s spectrum was deconvoluted into two components (Fig. 3b). The peak at 531.2eV is attributed to CO bonds while the other peak at 532.4eV corresponds to CO bonds. These results were confirmed in the correponding FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3c), in which the strong COOH and CO signals were also observed. Large quantities of COOH groups found at the CDs’ surface are likely to arise from the CC and CC bonds in coal being broken down by OH during the reaction leading to more periphery carbons, which were simultaneously oxidized into the COOH groups [35]. The higher O content of CDs (34.22wt%) than the as-received coal (15.87wt%) also suggests the presence of OH-induced oxidizing exfoliation for coal powders.

Our research also demonstrates the applicability of these synthesized CDs for the photocatalytic decomposition of organic materials, which is a global problem nowadays as pollutants such as dyes from industry are finding their way into water streams and disastrously affecting aquatic life and the environment [34,40,41]. Methylene blue (MB) and methyl orange (MO) are both fairly stable organic dyes under visible light irradiation without a photocatalyst and exhibit almost no photolysis [41]. The photocatalystic activity of CDs was evaluated by measuring the decomposition rate of MB and MO in water under visible light and compared with that of a commercial TiO2 photocatalyst (P25). Their adsorption ability towards dye molecules was measured under dark conditions similar to the reference [42]. The concentraion changes of MO soultion in the CDs and P25 were given in Fig. S14. No obvious adsorption was observed on CDs while ca. 11% of MO was adsorbed by P25. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the photocatalytic degradation rate for MO and MB under visible light was 0.135min−1 and 0.115min−1 respectively for our CDs, which is significantly faster than that of P25 (0.012min−1 and 0.045min−1 for MO and MB respectively). This result implies that the photocatalytic activity of CDs is several times higher than commercial photocatalysts (P25), which is made from elements that are scarcer and more costly than coal-derived carbon. Furthermore, CDs still exhibited high photoactivity comparing with P25 even though they had been reused three times (Fig. S15). According to our presented method, coal-based CDs can be produced inexpensively on a large scale from abundant, cheap stating materials using an environmentally friendly process without the burden of secondary pollution. Arguably this is a very significant advance to apply them for the photodegradation of pollutants using visible light without the need of an external UV source.

We also use TA to investigate the efficacy of the coal-based CDs during the photodegradation of MB and MO. Photocatalysis involves the generation of charge carriers, i.e. excess electrons (e−) and hole (h+), and the catalytic reactions induced by these species [40]. When CDs are excited with photons with energy higher than the bandgap, electrons are excited from their ground state (Fig. 4c). Consequently, the photogenerated electrons and holes could migrate to the surface of CDs after separation and result in the formation of OH, which can degrade and mineralize organic pollutants [34]. To examine whether OH radicals were formed or not, experiments were conducted in the presence of TA under the visible light irradiation. Fig. 4d shows the fluorescent signals associated with TAOH under visible-light irradiation of CDs and P25 suspended in TA solution for different irradiation times. It can be seen that CDs have a greater ability to generate OH than P25, suggesting they possess higher photocatalystic activity.

Basically, the ability of CDs to undergo photogenerated charge transfer to dye species on its surface is governed by their surface energy trap levels. Our previous works have proved that the surface groups of CDs can induce different energy trap levels, resulting in the different photocatalytic activities [6,18,43,44]. Because the pH change of the reaction solution could influence the surface energy trap levels of CDs, the photodegradation efficiencies of MB versus pH values under the same time irradiation was performed employing CDs as the photocatalysts. The photocatalytic activities of CDs for the MB degradation increase with increasing pH value (Fig. S16). This result is in record with our previous report [18].

In conclusion, we have demonstrated a green, mild, facile, and inexpensive method to prepare fluorescent CDs from coal using the selective oxidation of H2O2 for organic carbon in coal. This method has a significantly high yield and large potential in large-scale production of CDs using coal, which is one of the most abundant and low cost materials on the planet. The prepared CDs show excellent photocatalytic activity using only visible light and possess extremely promising attributes for application in the photodegradation of organic pollutants without the burden of secondary pollution. This work could open up the new use of coal that does not involve burning.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125, 51272301, 51172214, 51502270), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (Nos. 2012M510788, 2013T60269), Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths (2014021008), 131 Talent Plan of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.04.038.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Material characterization method and other additional results. The following is Supplementary data to this article:

A chemical method for identifying the photocatalytic active sites on carbon dots
